---
title: "ENVS-193DS_homework-03-DOC"
author: "Megan Zhao"
date: 5/28/26
format: pdf
toc: true
editor: source
messages: false
warning: false
---

# Set Up

Packages

```{r}
#| label: packages

# reading in packages
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(janitor)
library(readxl)

```

Data: 

```{r}
#| label: data

# creating object from 'kelp' from data ** come back to change this for hw and midterm lowkey
kelp <- read.csv(here('data', 'temp-kelp.csv'))

```

Cleaning:

```{r}
#| label: cleaning

# *** do we need to clean the data?

```

Aesthetics

```{r}
#| label: aesthetics

theme_set(theme_bw())

```



# Problem 1

## a.

[The appropriate tests to determine the strength of the relationship between temperature and giant kelp frond elongation rate are the Pearson's correlation test and the Spearman rank correlation test. While both tests can be used to determine relationship strength, Pearson's correlation is parametric, requiring continuous and normally distributed variables with independent observations, and explores the linear relationship between variables. On the other hand, the Spearman rank correlation is non-parametric, requiring independent observations, and explores the monotonic relationship between variables.]

## b.

```{r}
#| label: test-1-visualization

ggplot(
  data = kelp,
  mapping = aes(x = temp_c,
                y = kelp_elong)
) +
  geom_point(color = 'chartreuse4',
             alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(x = 'Ocean Temperature (°C)',
       y = 'Giant Kelp Frond Elongation Rate (cm day ***CHANGE THIS)')

```

## c.

### assumption checking 

```{r}
#| label: assumption-checking

cor.test(kelp$kelp_elong, kelp$temp_c,
         method = 'spearman')


```

[insert text here]

### test running

```{r}
#| label: test-running

```

## d.

[]

## e.

[]

## f.

```{r}
#| label: test-2-visualization

```

# Problem 2

## a.

```{r}
#| label: plot-1

```

```{r}
#| label: plot-2

```

## b.

[]

# Problem 3

## a.

[An affective visualization for my personal data could take the form of a drawing, showing box plots -- representing mood -- that are being climbed/conquered by a person. For example, if a good mood leads to more work being completed, an affective visualization could show a raised large stack of papers (higher box plot) with sticky tabs sticking out (overlaid data) and a confident/smiling person lifting them above their head. On the inverse, if a bad mood leads to less work being completed, an affective visualization could show strewn papers on the floor (lower box plot) with balled up papers strewn about (overlaid data) and a dejected/tired person sitting down on them. Ideally this visualization would make the viewer understand the feeling of being unproductive/unwell in terms of work completion.]

## b. 

[insert image]

## c.

[insert image]

## d. 

[]

## e.

[insert hyperlink]

# Problem 4

## a. 

[]

## b.

[]

## c.

[]

## d.

[]
